coredata.dc:title: Theranostic carbon dots ‘clathrate-like’ nanostructures for targeted photo-chemotherapy and bioimaging of cancer
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Pandey, Sunil
Gedda, Ganga Raju
Thakur, Mukeshchand
Bhaisare, Mukesh Lavkush
Talib, Abou
Khan, M. Shahnawaz
Wu, Shou-Mei
Wu, Hui-Fen
coredata.dc:description: The drug delivery application of carbon-based quantum dots is a new platform in the medical field. We report a unique morphological structure called carbon-dots clathrates (C-dotsCL) conjugated with folic acid (FA) for targeted delivery of methotrexate (MTX), an anti-cancer drug under physiological conditions followed by photothermal (PTT) and photodynamic (PDT) treatment on cancer cells. Under physiological milieu, the C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex are pH-dependent and it can control release the anti-cancer drug in vitro. Furthermore, in vivo studies were also performed by using genetically-induced pancreatic cancer model to comprehend the in vivo pharmacokinetics and tissue distribution pattern of C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex. The results demonstrated that the CQD was able to deliver high concentration of MTX in tumour tissues in contrast to the non-tumour tissues. The impact of near-infrared (NIR) laser (1064nm; Nd-YAG) on the CQDs system was found to be extremely effective for the rapid release of MTX in tumour tissues. The generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by C-dotsCL upon irradiation of NIR laser was evaluated using flow cytometry. After conjugation with the C-dotsCL, the half-life of the MTX drug (t1/2), elimination constant (Kel), area under the curve (AUC) was drastically improved to prove the efficacy of C-dotCL as a powerful drug vehicle. This study has significantly improved the cancer theranostics research using C-dotsCL to act as a multi-functional nanomaterial for imaging and as a drug delivery agent.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots clathrates
Methotrexate
Photothermal
Reactive oxygen species
Elimination constant
Area under curve

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Drug loading efficiency (DLE) and drug release
Free reactive oxygen radical analysis and bioimaging
Drug loading and drug release studies
Organ distribution and in vivo tumour toxicity studies

item-info.ce:copyright: The Korean Society of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing (a) synthesis and principle of formation of porous C-dotsCL complex and (b) loading of MTX drug and attachment of FA as a targeting agent.

Optical properties of C-dotsCL before and after complex formation. (a) UV–vis spectra of as-synthesized C-dotsCL (80μg/ml) and C-dotsCL–MTX conjugate. (b) Showing photoluminescence of C-dotsCL at different excitation wavelengths showing excitation dependent fluorescence property.

The morphological features showing TEM images of C-dotsCL formed (a–c) and (d) selective area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of amorphous clathrates-like nanoparticles, (e) DLS showing size distribution of C-dotsCL (f) the elemental analysis of EDS.
The photothermal properties of the C-dotsCL with respect to enhancement in temperature with respect to concentration.

In vitro drug release behaviour of MTX following (a) pH-responsive drug release and (b) pH-cum-NIR laser (1064nm) induced drug release as a function of time. Cumulative percentage of MTX released with and without laser at different pH values are shown in (c) and (d) respectively.

(a) Temperature profile obtained by increasing concentration of purified C-dotsCL as a function of irradiation time. (b) MTT-based assay showing percentage cell viability with and without exposure to NIR-irradiation on Vero and BCSCs HMLERsh E-cadherins at different concentration of C-dotsCL after 24h. (c) C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex with shown in units of equivalent concentration of MTX (μM). (d) Showing reactive oxygen species (ROS) production as observed as a function of increase in the fluorescence intensity of dihydrorhodamine dye (DHR) upon excitation at 450nm and emission was measured at 530nm, (n=3, mean±SD).

ROS study using flow cytometry using H2DCFDA dye in cancer cells. (a) Untreated cells served negative control (−) while (b) H2O2-treated cells served positive control (+). Cells treated with C-dotsCL and only laser did not show any significant ROS (c, d). However, cells treated with only MTX drug and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex showed increase in ROS level (e, f). Finally, the cells treated with C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex in the presence of laser showed relatively more ROS showing photodynamic efficiency of the complex.

Bioimaging and morphological analysis using confocal microscopic images of live BCSCs. Cells treated with (a–c) C-dotsCL only, (d–f) C-dotsCL+laser (13min), (g–i) C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex alone, (j–l) C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex+laser (13min). Images taken by excitation at 488nm and emission at 520nm.

(a) Serum distribution profile of free/bare MTX and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex, (b) human erythrocyte RBCs hemolysis of free/bare MTX, free FA, C-dotsCL and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex, (n=3, mean±SD). (c) Pictorial snapshots showing lysis/or otherwise of RBCs in the supernatant while intact RBCs form a pellet. (d) Organ distribution studies for a period of 6, 12 and 24h for the presence of MTX (nM) as free/bare MTX injected and in (e) C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex per gram body weight injected group. (f) Showing decrement in the tumor volume (mm3) measured over 17days period with respect to free MTX as well as C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex injected and subjected to NIR exposure (n=3, mean±SD). (g) The final weight of tumors post-treatment procedures calculated after 17days (n=3, mean±SD) and (h) showing their respective pictorial snapshots at day 1 and day 17 respectively.
Pharmacokinetic parameters after intravenous injection of MTX in experimental mice models.

head.ce:title: Theranostic carbon dots ‘clathrate-like’ nanostructures for targeted photo-chemotherapy and bioimaging of cancer

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Doctoral Degree Program in Marine Biotechnology
National Sun Yat-Sen University and Academia Sinica

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry and Center for Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, National Sun Yat-Sen University, 70, Lien-Hai Road, Kaohsiung 80424, Taiwan. Fax: +886 7 5253909.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The drug delivery application of carbon-based quantum dots is a new platform in the medical field. We report a unique morphological structure called carbon-dots clathrates (C-dotsCL) conjugated with folic acid (FA) for targeted delivery of methotrexate (MTX), an anti-cancer drug under physiological conditions followed by photothermal (PTT) and photodynamic (PDT) treatment on cancer cells. Under physiological milieu, the C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex are pH-dependent and it can control release the anti-cancer drug in vitro. Furthermore, in vivo studies were also performed by using genetically-induced pancreatic cancer model to comprehend the in vivo pharmacokinetics and tissue distribution pattern of C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex. The results demonstrated that the CQD was able to deliver high concentration of MTX in tumour tissues in contrast to the non-tumour tissues. The impact of near-infrared (NIR) laser (1064nm; Nd-YAG) on the CQDs system was found to be extremely effective for the rapid release of MTX in tumour tissues. The generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) by C-dotsCL upon irradiation of NIR laser was evaluated using flow cytometry. After conjugation with the C-dotsCL, the half-life of the MTX drug (t1/2), elimination constant (Kel), area under the curve (AUC) was drastically improved to prove the efficacy of C-dotCL as a powerful drug vehicle. This study has significantly improved the cancer theranostics research using C-dotsCL to act as a multi-functional nanomaterial for imaging and as a drug delivery agent.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past several decades, cancer has been an atrocious disease and need an utmost attention to decrease the tremendous rate of mortality. Unpredictable bio-physiological and molecular biological alterations during the onset of progression of the cancers make their diagnosis and treatments extremely intimidating task [1]. One of the major concerns in cancer chemotherapeutics is the delivery of the active pharmaceuticals within the microenvironment of the tumours [2] principally due to the altered or aberrated surrounding. These aberrations result due to metabolic and structural changes that occur during cancerous transformation making the cancer therapy difficult task [3,4].

With the advancement of medical nanotechnology, novel nanoparticle became crowned materials for diagnosis and treatment of a variety of cancers [5]. Carbon dots (C-dots) [6] has been a powerful material for delivery of drugs to the solid tumour [7]. Particles in nano-regimes can evade cardinal issues such as poor mobilisation of the drugs to body fluids, killing non-specific innocent cells (mainly due to lack of targeting), tumour resistance to anti-cancer drugs, permeation and retention (PR effect) of drugs in the cancerous milieu [8]. Recently, C-dots are favourite materials for biological applications due to their unique properties such as inherent fluorescence, extremely high biocompatibility and facile protocols for their synthesis [9–16].

These unique properties allowing the C-dots to be emerged as an alternative to the conventional hostile metal-containing semiconductor quantum dots such as CdTe, CdS and CdSe QDs [10]. The strong absorption of light in near-infrared (NIR) is advantageous for photothermal therapy (PTT) of solid tumours [17,18]. Also, their intense fluorescence and high quantum yield enables them to be applied for biological imaging [13,19,20].

Synthesis of porous carbon particles can be an alternative to this provided certain issues such as aggregation in the solution and larger size (c.a. 200nm) and considerable high hydrophobicity which makes their administration in the cells almost impossible [21]. Most of the protocols available for synthesis of porous carbon nanomaterials involve a cumbersome template-assisted method in multiple steps [22,23]. Unlike mesoporous silica, a widely-used nanomaterial for drug delivery, porous carbon nanomaterials can have higher surface areas and pore size along with lower inimical effects on biological systems [24,25].

We report a novel fluorescent mesoporous C-dots clathrate (C-dotsCL) using cetyl-trimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) as a templating agent using hydrothermal pyrolysis of widely used precursor citric acid. In contrast to the porous carbon nanoparticles which often pose two fundamental issues: first, relative hydrophobicity and second, non-uniformity in size and shape, C-dotsCL were found to be extremely dispersible in water and their size was also confined to <100nm. On the surface of these porous C-dotsCL, a potential anticancer drug methotrexate (MTX) was attached along with folic acid (FA) as a targeting molecule to make C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex. Due to the high affinity of FAs towards folic acid receptors (FARs) overexpressed on a variety of cancer cells, the FA–FAR interaction is one of most widely used targeting strategies in cancer drug delivery [3]. MTX is a widely used antineoplastic drug widely used for the treatment of advanced human malignancies related with acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, osteosarcomas, breast cancers and metastatic lymphomas [26]. The MTX is an antagonist of FA (an essential pre-requisite for DNA synthesis) and is considered as a potential therapeutic drug against many types of cancers having an overabundance of FARs on their surfaces. This C-dotsCL has an unique feature of high absorbance in NIR regime, which is most penetrating and safe radiation that can be used for photothermal therapy of cancer cells [13,27]. The cell cytotoxicity as performed on breast cancer stem cells (BCSCs), which often remain as a major reason for the recurrences of cancers after chemotherapy or surgery [28]. Furthermore in vivo biological distribution of drug and regression of tumour mass was analysed following photothermal and chemotherapy treatment.

All the solvents used in this experiment were of high purity and used as per instruction of materials data safety sheets. The CTAB, MTX, and poly (ethylene glycol)-2-aminoethyl ether were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). The breast cancer stem cells (HMLERsh E-cadherins) were kindly provided by Prof. Robert Weinberg, MIT and American Cancer Society, USA. The Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium was purchased from Thermo Scientific, USA (DMEM, HyClone® containing 4mM/l l-glutamine, 4500mg/l glucose) fetal bovine serum (FBS) were purchased from Gibco, USA. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS), trypsin-EDTA solution and pen-strep solution were purchased from Lonza, Belgium. 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFDA) was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich, USA.

Spectral properties of the C-dotsCL were studied by UV–vis–NIR spectrophotometer (Lambda-25, PerkinElmer, USA) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). The morphological details of C-dots were using High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; Zeiss Microimaging GmbH, Germany). The X-ray Diffraction was carried out using XRD; Phillips. The Raman spectra were recorded using Jobin–Yvon Labram spectrometer and the Fourier Transformed Infrared (FTIR) Spectroscopy (Brucker) studies were performed within the spectral window 500–4000cm−1. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential studies (pH 6.5–7) were performed using Malvern Zetasizer.

For the synthesis of C-dotsCL, 2g of citric acid was dissolved in 10ml of poly (ethylene glycol) 2-amino ethyl ether and mixed well by ultrasonication (Cole-Parmer 130-W Ultrasonic Processors) for 20min at ambient temperature (25±3°C). To this mixture, 0.1g of CTAB (100mM) was added followed by 5ml of NaOH (0.1N), under continuous stirring conditions for 1h. The mixture was sealed in a Teflon-coated container in a stainless-steel autoclave container and then heated at 200°C for 12h [13]. The resulting (clear) yellow coloured solution was washed or centrifuged (20,000rpm, 25min) thrice and the supernatant was discarded. This ensured the removal of almost all the unreacted CTAB. The solution was then passed through ammonium chloride saturated Sephadex G-100 column and eluted with nano-pure water for 8h to further remove any minute residual CTAB from C-dotsCL. An intense blue coloured fluorescence was exhibited by C-dotsCL upon observation under UV light (λ ex =365nm). This was considered as the most fundamental confirmation of C-dots [11–14,18]. The C-dotsCL powders were obtained by vacuum heating at 80°C for 12h and the appropriate concentration was obtained by dissolving them in nanopore water and the pH value of the final solution was maintained at pH 7.0 and stored at 4°C.

The loading of MTX onto CQDs was done by the following procedure. Briefly, 10ml of the purified C-dotsCL solution (200μg/ml) was mixed with 5ml of MTX (0.5M in 1M NaOH) and stirred for 3h at ambient temperature (25±3°C) under continuous nitrogen purging to avoid external interferences. The C-dotsCL-MTX solution was washed using nanopure water with multiple round of centrifugation (20,000rpm, 25min) to remove any unanchored MTX. The final solution was purified by dialysis against pure water (Mili Q, 18.2MΩ, USA) for 48h and freeze-dried.

The FA was added into 20ml of DMSO (1.25%) and the mixture was subjected to sonication for 1h. The carboxylate group of FA was activated by addition of n-hydroxysuccinamide (NHS, 225μg) and N,N-dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCC, 125μg) to the above solution. The reaction was performed in an inert environment by purging N2 gas at 25±2°C for 12h (FA/NHS/DCC molar ratio was kept at 2:2:1). Attachment of FA is facilitated by adding 15ml of C-dotsCL–MTX and 5ml of activated FA (3:0.1w/v) and the solution was further subjected to purging with N2 gas (6h) under constant stirring using magnetic stirrer (150rpm) and further incubated in dark for 24h. The resultant solution was centrifuged at 20,000rpm for 25min. The pellet was re-dissolved in appropriate volume of nanopure water and stored at −20°C. Free FA was removed using a 3000kDa dialysis bag against phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH 7.2). The post-dialyzed samples were centrifuged at 20,000rpm for 25min at 20°C. The pellet was re-dialyzed against deionized water for 24h under continuous stirring and the concentrate was obtained by freeze-drying for 12h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading efficiency (DLE) and drug release
ce:section.ce:para:
The DLE was calculated by Eq. (1) [29]. Typically, 5ml of the drug attached complex was centrifuged at 6000rpm for 15min at 20°C and dialysed against nanopure water to remove the unbound drug. Absorbance (at 280nm) readings were taken after 24h.
(1)
D L E =
Theoretical amount of drug loaded − Free drug
Theoretical amount of drug loaded
× 100

Briefly, 5ml of purified C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex was placed in two different dialysis tubing. The drug release study was conducted at 37°C with continuous stirring at 100rpm in two different buffer systems containing 0.1% BSA (pH 5.8 and 7.4). To measure the MTX release content, samples (1ml) were periodically removed and replaced with an equivalent volume of the phosphate buffer solution containing bovine serum albumin (0.1%). The amount of released MTX was analysed using a UV–vis spectrophotometer at 280nm. For photothermal drug release study, C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex solution at similar pH values was irradiated with NIR laser pulse (λ =1064nm, Minilite continuum, USA). The release study was conducted at pH 5.8 and 7.4 by centrifuging at 10,000rpm for 10min. The supernatant was then analysed for drug release by recording the absorbance at 280nm. All experiments were performed at least in triplicates t ensure the reproducibility.

The HMLERsh E-cadherins cells were seeded at a density of 4000 cells/well in a 96 well-plate cultured in DMEM medium (8.5% fetal bovine serum and 0.5% penicillin–streptomycin mixture) at the standard conditions of 5% CO2 at 37°C. For evaluating cytotoxicity, an MTT-based assay was done and cytotoxicity was measured spectrophotometrically at 570nm after 24h. For an in vitro photo-chemotherapy, BCSCs were treated with C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex for 8h. The cells were then irradiated by NIR laser (1064nm) using a pulse wave Nd-YAG Laser, Minilite continuum Inc. USA in a mini quartz cuvette. The enhancement in temperature was recorded by connected to temperature recorder. The efficiency of photo-conversion was computed by previous methods [30,31].

Following equations were used for calculation of photothermal efficiency (PE):
(2) Qabsorbance/Qtotal ×100
where Qabsorbance and Qtotal is, the energy absorbed by the varying concentrations of C-dotsCL and total laser energy irradiated to the samples respectively. The Qtotal was calculated as a function of laser intensity, and irradiation time. The Eq. (3) is used to calculate Qabsorbance:
(3) Qabsorbance =[(MC-dot CL ×CpC-dots CL) +(MW ×CpW)]×(ΔT)
where MC-dots CL and Cp C-dots CL are the concentration (in moles) of carbon in C-dotsCL and heat capacity of carbon respectively. The MW and CpW are molar concentration and heat capacity of water used as solvent respectively. The Qabsorbance was expressed in Joules. The ΔT is the change in the temperature after 13min of laser irradiation.

The Quinine sulphate (QY, Ф=58%) at the excitation wavelength (λ ex) of 250nm was used as a reference for calculating QY for C-dotsCL, which was calculated using following formula (Eq. (4)):
(4) ФS=ФR [AR (λ R) n2 S IS]/[AS (λ S) n2 R IR]
ФS and ФR are quantum yields of sample and reference respectively; AR (λ R) and AS (λ S) are the absorbance of the reference and sample at excitation wavelengths; nS and nR are refractive indices of the sample medium and reference medium respectively; IS and IR are the integrated fluorescence intensities of sample and reference respectively. The elemental percentage composition of C, H, O, N was found to be 53.21±4.23, 8.21±1.2, 18.26±4.56 and 20.32± 4.44 respectively.

Healthy ICR nude mice (Lasco, Charles River Technology, Taiwan) weighing 25–28g, kept at a clean sterile condition at ambient temperature were used for comprehending the biological distribution of the MTX and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex. The mice were kept under folate-free diet for 15days prior to the experiments. The experimental protocols involving animal model were approved by the review committee of National Sun Yat-Sen University (The animal and use committee, 2015–2016). The animals were handled under the ethical guidelines provided by this organization. All experiments were performed in accordance with the ethical norms of National Sun Yat-Sen University. The tumour growth was instigated by injecting 70μl of HLMERshE−cadherin cells (1×104 cells/ml in Hanks Balanced salt solution) in the right flank of the mice. The tumours were allowed to grow to the volume of 300mm3 prior to the PTT and PDT experiments. At stipulated time periods, the mice were weighed as well the size of the tumour was determined using Vernier calliper.

The tumour volume was studied using following formula elsewhere [29]:
(5) Tumour volume= π (length×width×height)/6

The nude mice were divided into three sets (named S I, S II and S III) and each set contained five mice for injection of CQDs complexes. The SI, SII were injected with bare MTX (300μl of 0.5mM MTX) and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA solution (MTX equivalent concentration) respectively via the tail vein. Group S III served as a control. The blood was drawn from each set of mice at regular interval of time and analysed for MTX content spectroscopically. The mice were sacrificed; their organs were removed and minced in PBS for analysis of the drug concentrations inside various mice organs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Free reactive oxygen radical analysis and bioimaging
ce:section.ce:para: To compute the production of free radicals by C-dotsCL, p-nitroso-N, N′-dimethyl aniline (RNO) was used as a chemical indicator along with histidine. Briefly, 1ml of C-dotsCL and saline (as control) was blended with RNO solution (250μM, 150μl) and histidine (30mM, 300μl) solution. This solution (300μl) was irradiated then with the Nd-YAG laser (λ =1064nm, Minilite Continuum, USA). The solution was studied spectroscopically at 440nm using an UV–vis spectroscopy (Thermo, Evolution, USA). For internal cellular ROS analysis using flow cytometry, the BCSCs were loaded with respective nanoparticles (with or without laser). The cells were harvested and treated with (DCFDA, 20μM) in 200μl PBS (pH 7) and analysed using flow cytometry (at least 1000 events were calculated) in FITC filter. Live cell confocal bioimaging was performed by treating cells with C-dotsCL, C-dotsCL–MTX–FA, and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex. The treated cells were excited at 488nm and emission was recorded at 520nm.

Human blood was collected via veins and stored in EDTA vials (18mg EDTA BD VacutainerF®, UK). The red blood cells (RBCs) were collected by centrifuging the whole blood and suspended in PBS, at pH 7.2. In order to compare the percentage (%) of hemolysis, 3ml of RBCs were suspended in PBS (negative control) and distilled water (positive control). One millilitre of diluted bare MTX (0.5mM) and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex (equivalent drug concentration) was incubated with 3ml of RBCs and the total volume was made to 10ml using PBS for dilution. These suspensions were studied separately and compared with hemolysis produced by bare MTX. All the above formulations were incubated at 37°C for 20min followed by centrifugation at 4000rpm for 7min. The saline as regarded as a blank and the absorbance of the supernatant were studied at 540nm using an UV–vis spectroscopy (Thermo, USA). The extent of hemolysis was calculated with respect to distilled water (considered 100% hemolysis).

All experiments were performed at least 3 times and presented as mean standard deviation (SD). The differences in study groups were evaluated using the Student’s t-test, which was considered statistically significant if p <0.05, and highly significant if p <0.01.

In the synthesis of fluorescent C-dotsCL, the role of CTAB was found to be very pivotal. During the hydrothermal synthesis of C-dotsCL using citric acid and poly (ethylene glycol) 2-amino ethyl ether complex, CTAB due to its micelle forming property, acted as a novel template and played a crucial role in shaping the structure to a clathrate. Due to surface orchestration with amino PEG and CTAB, the zeta potential of the final complex was measured to be +13mV at pH 6.5. The process is explained in the schematic illustration of synthesis and surface modification of C-dotsCL (Fig. 1 ). Due to this, C-dotCL became porous to some extent and was exploited as high capacity drug container. There were following considerable properties, which confirmed the synthesis of C-dotsCL:
i
Emission of bright blue fluorescence under UV light (λ ex =365nm).
ii
Signature absorption in UV regime at 250 and 350nm due to specific electron transitions associated with the structural as well surface properties of C-dotsCL (Fig. 2 a).
iii
Typical excitation dependent emission of visible light between 250 to 350nm (Fig. 2b).
iv
Ultra-small size ranging from 20–50nm as confirmed by HRTEM explains differential size distribution. The shape of the carbon dots was found to be like protein clathrates used to deliver payloads to the biological cells.

The origin of fluorescence is due to surface encouraged defects in structure leading to the generation of carbons which different states of hybridization [29] and corresponding multiple emissions [13,33].

Peaks in UV regime are generated due to different excited states of π electrons associated with C-dots. Both the peaks at 250 and 350nm are originated from the π→π* and n→π* transitions of the electrons [34]. The most peculiar characteristic of the C-dotsCL was found to be absorbance in near infrared regime (600 and 1000nm). This property has a profound influence in the photothermal application and hyperthermia-based cytotoxicity to cancerous cells in the tumour microenvironment by the process called photothermal therapy [13,35,36]. Furthermore, the C-dots was found to be the excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 2b). After excitations of C-dots clathrates at 250, 300, 400 and 600 with the emissions were obtained at higher wavelengths. The PL property is one the most burgeoning property of C-dots which can be possibly exploited in sensors, biological imaging and actuator [12,13], the problem being the low quantum yield unlike conventional semiconductors quantum dots at higher wavelengths.

The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images show differential structures with few ranging 2–5nm and others in systematic clathrates form 20–30nm (Fig. 3 a–c) with an irregular atomic arrangement (Fig. 3d). The energy dispersive analysis (EDS) showed major composition as carbon and few other residual elements (Fig. 3e). C-dotsCL was found to be semi-crystalline nature that was further evident from the XRD spectra (Fig. S1a). Signature diffraction peaks at 2θ =32° and 41° instigate from the diffraction patterns of (002) and (101) planes of graphitic carbon respectively [38]. The bonding properties of C-dotsCL were investigated using Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectrum exhibited peaks located at 1325cm−1 and another at 1583cm−1 (Fig. S1b) similar to the previous report [12,13]. The peak at 1325cm−1 is called D band. The D-band is originated due to defects and disordered carbon materials (mainly carbon soot) which played a significant role in the formation of C-dotsCL during hydrothermal pyrolysis at higher temperature (200°C). On the other hand, a second peak at 1583cm−1 (called G band) signifies graphitization of the carbon materials. The ratio of the two bands (ID/IG) was calculated to be 0.46 which describe the degree of graphitization of carbon nanomaterials. A higher ratio of the both the bands indicates the presence of amorphous carbon in the C-dotsCL [13,39,40]. The surface interaction chemistry of bare C-dotsCL [7,17] and other complexes are shown in Fig. S2 where an attachment chemistry is explained. The QY obtained were 4.5±0.91%. The QY is lower compared to reported literature reports [41–45]. Plausible implications for increase in the QY of C-dots include higher external polarity and protocity of the medium along with its H-bonding characteristics [44]. In addition, recently, molecular origin of the citric acid-derived C-dots (using different strategy) have been attribed to the formation of different organic-derivatives as fluorescing units albeit at the core of the C-dots [44,45]. The high QY was attributed to the increase in hydrogen-bonding and π–π stacking within the individual C-dots. Thus both — the abundance of fluorescing units (or lower carbon-content) and the H-bonding characteristics (which is a consequence of solvent) are responsible for the determination of QY in C-dots. In C-dotsCL, aggregation of individual C-dots units have been observed to form C-dotsCL (see Fig. 3a,c) which could be another reason for low QY. Aggregation or collision between C-dots have been shown to have decrease in the QY due to loss of energy [47]. Furthermore, the C-dotsCL have been capped with a polymer like PEG to increase its solubility and stability. Therefore, it could be possible that the C-dotsCL could behave like recently discovered non-conjugated polymer dots as they have similar amorphicity, aggregation characteristics, as well as functional groups (e.g. CO or CN) [47,48]. All these reasons from solvent function to inter-molecular interaction can in ensemble affect the QY of the C-dotsCL as a whole. However, C-dotsCL nano-asssembly showed novel structural arrangement and excellent photothermal characteristics as seen in Fig. 4 .

After the exposure of the laser for 13min, the corresponding changes in the photothermal conversion efficiency, calculated by using Eqs. (2) and (3). The most optimal concentration of C-dotsCL was found to be 80μg/ml. With regards to the physiological temperature, there was an enhancement of temperature to 17°C at 80μg/ml (Fig. 4). After this concentration, there was no apparent change in the temperature with respect to time. The maximum photothermal conversion in the above case was recorded to be 42.67% as shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and drug release studies
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most appreciable properties of C-dotsCL was found to exhibit extremely high drug loading capacity. The interaction of MTX with the surface of modified C-dotsCL was mainly due to electrostatic interaction of positive charge present on the surface of C-dotsCL. However, other types of weak interactions may also be present. Using Eq. (1), the loading efficiency of the C-dotsCL was found to be ∼97%. This is the highest concentrations of drug loading capacity reported of the various of drug delivery vehicles based on carbon nanomaterials [49]. High drug loading capacity of C-dotsCL is due to high porosity as well as the unique clathrate-like structural arrangement. Time-dependent release of the MTX under mimicked physiological milieu under pH values 7.4 and 5.8 is shown in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a,b displays concentration of MTX released at different pH with and without NIR laser irradiation. Sustained drug release behaviour pattern was obtained till plateau was achieved over 72h periods at respective pH values. However, upon NIR-Irradiation, drug release was faster and when compared with the non-irradiated counterpart, the equivalent amount of drug release was obtained within 260min. Here, the release was a synergistic effect of both laser-induced drug release as well as the impact of the pH as depicted by the variance in the drug release curve at different pH values (Fig. 5b).

Under complete absence of NIR laser exposure (Fig. 5c), 2.2% MTX was found to be released in 2h at pH 5.8 which got boosted to 14% in 8h. Finally, 100% MTX was fired at the end of 72h. However, under NIR-Irradiated conditions (Fig. 5d), initially there was mere 5.7% release till 10min, which then approached to ∼45% at 50min and 100% release, was found after 260min. At physiological pH 7.4, initially the amount of drug release was faster as compared to pH 5.8 without laser irradiation. However, under laser irradiation their release was relatively slower than in the acidic pH, which confirmed that the C-dotsCL is a pH-responsive drug delivery system [50]. After the cancer transformation, pH of the tumour microenvironment becomes slightly acidic due to high metabolism, which initiated rapid cascade of errors leading to mutation, hyper angiogenesis and hence metastatic form of cancer [51]. This type of drug delivery vehicles can release high amount of drug after reaching to tumour milieu resulting in their high retention for enhanced efficacy.

The photothermal property of C-dotsCL was measured by analyzing the increase in temperature using Bi-Sb thermocouple connected to temperature recorder upon irradiating of 1064nm laser for varying time duration. Fig. 6 a shows an increase in the temperature upon increasing irradiation time. The solution containing no C-dotsCL was taken as a control. The distance between the laser probe and the test solution was kept constant throughout for maintaining consistency in the experiments. It was found that there was a linear relationship between laser irradiation time and temperatures with higher nanoparticle concentration. The reason behind the photothermal property of C-dotsCL was due to the high photothermal conversion efficiency (after 13min of exposure using 1064nm Nd:YAG laser), particularly at higher concentrations (>60μg/ml) as shown in Table 1 earlier. Similar findings were reported with graphene-based quantum dots which are partially crystalline materials [13]. However, the photothermal activity of unique clathrate-like carbon nanoparticles is not elucidated yet, to the best of our knowledge. In contrast to previous reports, current nanoparticle systems provide flexibility in terms of higher drug loading and responsive to wide-range of NIR lasers due to higher absorption in the NIR wavelength.

The inimical effect of C-dotsCL and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA on Vero cells and HLMERsh E-cadherin (BCSCs) were studied to validate the role of the photothermal property of C-dotsCL (Fig. 6). Cytotoxicity profile showed synergistic effect of photo-chemotherapy on mammalian cell lines. As a control, Vero cells were used to show the targeting potential of C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex. Without irradiation, bare C-dotsCL (10–80μg/ml) showed feeble cytotoxicity on both Vero and BCSCs (HMLERsh E-Cadherins). A maximum of 93.67±2.1% (On BCSCs) and 93.18±1.8% (on Vero cells) cell viability was obtained at 80μg/ml of C-dotsCL complex (Fig. 6b). This nature of cell viability was obtained due to the absence of the active targeting as well as exceptionally high biocompatibility nature of the C-dotsCL In contrast to above findings, upon NIR-Irradiation (1064nm NdYAG laser), an incredible change in the cell viability was observed (Fig. 6c). Cell viability decreased to 65±2.3% (On BCSCs) and 71±3.8% (On Vero cells) with 80μg/ml of C-dotsCL due to cytotoxic effect of photo-thermal activity of bare C-dotsCL (Fig. 6a). Another pivotal finding of C-dotsCL was found to be the generation of free radicals upon irradiation of NIR radiation. Variation in the effect on both cells might be attributed to variation in the degree of penetration activity of the nanoparticles as well as the reactive oxygen species (ROS) production upon irradiation from C-dotsCL (Fig. 6d), which was in accordance with previous reports [13].

In this regards, an inherent photothermal as well as photodynamic property of C-dotsCL was further exploited to analyse the activity of pristine MTX drug and the effect of folic acid upon selective targeting of BCSCs. The efficiency of the final complex (C-dotsCL–MTX–FA) showed promising in vitro results, highlighting selective targeting and photothermal cytotoxicty of BCSCs. In Fig. 6c, without irradiation, upon gradual increase in complex concentration (shown representative concentration of MTX) from 100nM to 1mM, the cell viability decreased to 41.16±2.05% (in BCSCs) and 68.35±3.41% (in Vero cells) at maximum concentration of 80μg/ml of representative fraction of C-dotsCL in the complex. The results are comparable to bare MTX cytotoxicity profile as depicted in Fig. S3. The difference in the cell viability is due to the availability of BCSC-specific tumour marker ligands folic acid which differentiated them from normal Vero cells. Upon irradiation (Fig. 5c), the percentage cell viability of the complex abrogated to 35±1.75% (in BCSCs) and 65±3.25% (in Vero cells). Combined chemotherapeutic potential of MTX along with photo-thermal activity of C-dotsCL acted upon in a pH-responsive as well irradiation responsive drug release. The drastic difference in the cell viability of BCSCs and Vero cells was attributed to the presence of folic acid, thus it is most widely used active targeting ligands in cancer therapies [52]. It must also be noted that ROS production from C-dotsCL further contributed to the cytotoxicity upon photo-irradiation in synergy. The MTX alone could act as a generator of reactive oxygen species in physiological conditions as per previous reports [53] and hence could be another factor for cell Cytotoxicity. Inhibitory concentration (IC50) of the mesoC-dots–FA–MTX decreased from 0.55mM to 0.37mM without and with NIR-irradiation respectively on BCSCs.

We therefore, performed flow cytometry to quantitatively analyse the formation of ROS species in the BCSCs (Fig. 7 ). As shown in Fig. 7a,b, the untreated cells served as negative control (0% ROS) and the cells treated with H2O2 served as a positive control (100% ROS) as evident from the shift in the fluorescnece intensity profile, an indicator or ROS formation. In the same regards, the bare C-dotsCL (Fig. 7c) and only-laser treated cells (Fig. 7d) showed little or no ROS (<2%) in comparision to untreated cells. However, there was an increasing significant shift in the fluorescnece intensity (100% relative ROS) upon addition of pristine MTX drug (Fig. 7e), C-dotsCL–MTX–FA (Fig. 7f) and laser treated C-dotsCL–MTX complex (Fig. 7g) respectively. Clearly, an increase in the ROS production in C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex alone was due to the presence of MTX (Fig. 7e,f) whilst a further enhancement after laser irradiation was due to laser-induced release of the drug inside the cells (Fig. 7g). Similar ROS effects were studied elsewhere where for photothermal-cum-photodynamic cytotoxicty of breast cancer cells was studied by ROS production [13,54]. There are several reports of fluorescent carbon nanospheres to be exploited in harnessing solar spectrum for photothermal enhancement in applications such as photovoltaic solar cells [55]. It was found that the thermal capacity of carbon nanospheres was strongly influenced by the amount of the same in solution. Using the appropriate concentration, there was considerable enhancement in temperature from 30°C to 64°C after exposing them to solar spectrum. Our results are consistent with the above findings both in terms of concentration dependent photothermal therapy as well as generation of ROS inside cells.

Fig. 8 shows confocal microscopy of live cells of BCSCs after 24h. The cells were treated with C-dotsCL, C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex, NIR laser, and in combination. Fig. 8a–c shows proper cell morphology of cells having cytoplasmic intake of C-dotsCL as evident by the fluorescence of the cell cytoplasm. The untreated cells did not show any fluorescence (data not shown). Many carbon-based nanoparticles have been reported to enter the cells via endocytosis mechanism [56]. Upon laser irradiation, the cells treated with C-dotsCL showed slight deformation (Fig. 8d–f) and few C-dotsCL entering cell nucleus probably due to local thermal stress posed by C-dotsCL in the cytoplasm upon laser irradiation which changes the cellular internal pressure or viscosity. Furthermore, change in the cell morphology (round-like) was evident in C-dotsCL–MTX–FA treated cells (Fig. 8g–i) and laser irradiated C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex (Fig. 8j–l). This is due to the anti-proliferative effect of MTX drug and very high temperature rise due to laser irradiation to C-dotsCL complex. The synergistic activity of photothermal, chemotherapy, and photodynamic activity of laser-induced C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex, clearly demonstrates the cancer cell cytotoxicity. It is worth noticing that the C-dotsCL did not lose its fluorescence upon laser irradiation throughout unlike the limitations of organic dyes. In other words, it showed high photostability inside the cells which is very important property that has been used in post-irradiation studies on cancer or cell-tracking [13]. This property can be explored further for long-term tracking of cells or biomolecular sensing in vivo.

The efficiency of sustained release of MTX by C-dotsCL was substantiated by injecting the C-dotsCL–MTX–FA as well as MTX alone in 5–6 weeks ICR mice models weighing ∼28g. After injection of 50mM/kg of MTX and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex (equivalent concentration of MTX) via tail vein. The blood was aspirated and analysed for the presence of MTX using HPLC. In a stark contrast to bare MTX, the residence time of the MTX in plasma was highly improved when complexed with C-dotsCL and FA. The t1/2 of the MTX was found to be enhanced to 6.4h in comparison to plain MTX i.e. 2.6h. The C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex thus showed a longer circulation as compared to pristine drug (Fig. 9 a). Furthermore, since it is very important to understand the effect of nanoparticles on the blood components mainly red blood corpuscles (RBCs) to understand their toxicology and fate in the blood. Fig. 8a shows hemolysis assay showing effect of each component in terms of percentage hemolysis of RBCs. Positive control (Triton-X100, 10mM) represent 100% haemolysis whereas PBS (pH 7) represents negative control. Pristine (or free) MTX drug showed ca. 21% hemolysis, followed by free C-dotsCL (15%), C-dotsCL–MTX–-FA complex (7%) and free FA showed ca. (5%). C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex showed much less hemolysis activity as compared to bare MTX or C-dotsCL due to non-availability of charged functional moieties on the drug and the nanoparticle leading to hampering in the interaction with RBCs. This becomes an important parameter to understand as far as toxicology of nanoparticles based drug delivery systems are concerned. Fig. 9c shows hemolysis images of the corresponding blood supernatants (hemolyzed) and RBCs settled at the bottom.

In addition, other pharmacological parameters such as half-life (t1/2), elimination constant (Kel), Area under the curve (AUC0–24 and AUC0–inf), Volume of Distribution (Vd) and Mean Residence time (MRT) were studied. Details of the pharmoco-kinetic parameters are displayed in Table 2 . Another important parameter found to be improved after conjugation of the MTX with C-dotsCL was found to be decreased drug clearance rate from the plasma. Kel of MTX and C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex was found to be 0.133 and 0.072 respectively.
All the vales were statistically validated using student’s t-test (P <0.05 in comparison with bare MTX).

C-dotsCL was found to play cardinal role in improving the properties of MTX with respect to current problems in cancer therapeutics. Most of the anti-neoplastic drugs have short residence time and rapid clearance rate from the body leading to less efficacy [57]. Prolonged circulation of the drugs, increase its interaction of with the target such as solid tumours.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Organ distribution and in vivo tumour toxicity studies
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9d,e shows the ability of the C-dotsCL to distribute the drug over vital organs of the body and tumour at the time interval of 6, 12 and 24h. The concentration of bare MTX (Fig. 9d) was found to be low in solid tumour tissue and steeply enhanced after conjugation with C-dotsCL–FA–MTX complex (Fig. 9e). This was due to FA–FARs interaction, a widely-used targeting molecules for solid tumours due to wide spread expression of folic acid receptors on most of the cancer cells. Maximum accumulation of free MTX was observed in kidney as ∼26μMg−1 at 6h which reduced to ∼6μMg−1 after 24h (Fig. 9d). In C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex, kidney accumulation was ∼23μM at 6h which again subsided to ∼8μM after 24h (Fig. 9e). In other tissues, such as spleen, liver, brain and stomach, MTX concentration was found to be very low. After 24h, MTX was found to be ca. 3, 4, 0.2 and 7μMg−1 in spleen, liver, brain and stomach respectively after treatment with C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex, which was very low. Decrease in the concentration of MTX in reticulo-endothelial organs such as liver and spleen due to uptake by phagocytic cells.

The effect of photo-chemotherapy regime was found to be effective as calculated by tumour volume (mm3) for 17days (n=3) showed in Fig. 9f–h. Sterile saline (pH 7) was used as a control. It was found that there was no significant occurrence of tumour re-growth followed by therapy as compared to control groups. At the end of 17 days, the tumour volume of control was found to be 120mm3 whereas no relative significant increase in tumour was found in C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex injected mice, suggesting high regression of tumour (Fig. 9f). The weight of the tumour was found be decreased after administration of MTX and C-dotsCL–FA–MTX complexes for 17days (Fig. 9g). Fig. 9h is the pictorial presentation of tumour after initial and final days of treatment using the aforementioned nano-complex. Due to the impact of targeting, the efficacy of later was found to be high in order to reduce the tumour weight (mainly due to high accumulation in the tumour regime as discussed).

The clathrate-like C-dots were thus found to be an excellent anti-cancer drug carrier with high drug loading capacity as well as exceptionally high biocompatibility. The drug release under physiological conditions was found to be affected by surrounding pH of the medium as well as irradiation of NIR radiation. This laid the basis of chemo-photothermal properties of C-dotsCL for the treatments of tumours. Pharmacokinetic studies of the C-dotsCL–MTX–FA displayed the capacity of the drug delivery vehicle to enhance the half-life of MTX in the serum, thus increasing their mean residence time in the body with respect to bare MTX. Another peculiar property of C-dotsCL–MTX–FA complex was found to be its highly specific capacity to accumulate inside tumour microenvironment bypassing most of the vital organs such as heart and liver. Therefore, a complete ablation of tumours was achieved using synergistic photo-chemo therapy.

A future evaluation of C-dotsCL can be done and its multi-functional role can be explored particularly in biomedical sensing. Recently, many intracellular compounds and nanoparticles have been shown for sensing cellular biomolecules and ions [58], physical parameters like temperature [12], and toxic heavy compounds like cyanides [59]. With C-dotsCL, due to their unique structural arrangement in addition to their inherent fluorescence property, intracellular sensing can be carried out. They can thus help in biomolecular photoluminescence scanning or sensing inside cells.

In conclusion, we have reported for the first time, the carbon dots clathrate-like self-assembled units of carbon nanoparticles with high anti-cancer drug loading capacity. The C-dotsCL showed multi-functional properties viz., photo-responsiveness, photodynamic, inherent fluorescence and long term photostability. By actively targeting breast cancer cells we have studied its pharmacokinetics, organ bio-distribution and the complex was very potent in reducing the tumour growth in vivo. Thus, we see more studies and advancement particularly those involving biomolecular sensing in biomedical nanotechnology using their unique properties these novel nanostructures.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the financial support from the NSYSU-KMU Joint Research Project (#NSYSUKMU106-P027) for financial support. We also acknowledge the TEM assistance of Mr. Wang G. C. from National Sun Yat-Sen University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jiec.2017.06.008.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proc. Natl. Acad. Sci. U. S. A.
